reasoning: The probability that 2025 will be the hottest year on record is currently quite low as we head into the middle of the year. Following the unprecedented heat of 2024 (driven by a strong El Nino combined with global warming trends), global temperatures have begun to cool slightly as ocean conditions transition out of El Nino and potentially into weak La Nina territory. NOAA's statistical analysis, updated through April 2025, sets the probability of 2025 being the warmest year on record at just 3%. Furthermore, while January 2025 was the hottest January on record, the subsequent months (February, March, April) have consistently lagged behind the anomalies set in 2024. NASA's Gavin Schmidt and other climate centers generally project 2025 to be the second- or third-hottest year. Although Berkeley Earth maintained a much higher 34% probability estimate in its March update, their model likely still captures the residual high variance from the start of the year. Factoring in NOAA's 3% chance and the Polymarket price of 12% (which was likely pricing in early-year variance or lagging Berkeley's March data), a 6% estimate fairly values the current scientific consensus against the small remote possibility of an unexpected late-year warming surge.
